content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 32 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 116r–118r | **Source Images:** DEU_032_page_001.jpg, DEU_032_page_002.jpg, DEU_032_page_003.jpg, DEU_032_page_004.jpg, DEU_032_page_005.jpg, DEU_032_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_032_vaticanus_page_002.jpg, DEU_032_vaticanus_page_003.jpg

---

*[Ha'azinu — The Song of Moses. This is the great poem embedded at the heart of Deuteronomy, set out in the manuscript in two columns of verse. PNEUMA renders it with full poetic rhythm, breath, and force.]*

---

**1**
Listen, O heavens — let me speak!
    Earth, hear every word from my mouth!
**2**
Let my teaching fall like rain,
    let my words descend like dew —
like gentle showers on new grass,
    like heavy rain on tender plants.

**3**
I will proclaim the name of the LORD —
    ascribe greatness to our God!

**4**
The Rock! His work is perfect,
    for all his ways are just.
A faithful God who does no wrong —
    upright and righteous is he.

**5**
They have acted corruptly toward him;
    to their shame they are no longer his children —
they are a crooked and perverse generation.

**6**
Is this the way you repay the LORD,
    you foolish and senseless people?
Is he not your Father, your Creator,
    who made you and established you?

**7**
Remember the days of old,
    consider the years of generations past.
Ask your father and he will tell you,
    your elders and they will explain it.

**8**
When the Most High apportioned the nations,
    when he divided all humanity —
he set the boundaries of the peoples
    according to the number of the sons of God.

**9**
For the LORD's own portion is his people;
    Jacob is the share of his inheritance.

**10**
In a desert wasteland he found him,
    in a howling, empty wilderness.
He surrounded him, he cared for him,
    he guarded him like the apple of his eye.

**11**
Like an eagle rousing its nest,
    hovering over its young —
spreading its wings, catching them,
    bearing them on its pinions —

**12**
the LORD alone guided him;
    no foreign god was with him.

**13**
He made him ride on the heights of the land
    and fed him from the produce of the fields.
He nourished him with honey from the rock,
    and oil from the flinty crag —

**14**
curds from the herd and milk from the flock,
    with fat of lambs,
rams of Bashan and goats,
    with the finest of the wheat —
and from the foaming blood of the grape
    you drank your fill.

**15**
But Jeshurun grew fat and kicked —
    you grew fat, thick, and sleek.
He abandoned God who made him
    and rejected the Rock of his salvation.

**16**
They made him jealous with foreign gods,
    they provoked him with detestable idols.
**17**
They sacrificed to demons, not to God —
    to gods they had never known,
new gods who had just arrived,
    gods your ancestors never feared.

**18**
You ignored the Rock who fathered you;
    you forgot the God who gave you birth.

**19**
The LORD saw this and rejected them,
    because his sons and daughters had provoked him.
**20**
He said: "I will hide my face from them;
    I will see what becomes of them.
For they are a perverse generation —
    children who have no loyalty in them.

**21**
They made me jealous with what is no god;
    they provoked me with their worthless idols.
I will make them jealous with those who are no people;
    I will provoke them with a foolish nation.

**22**
For my anger has blazed into fire
    burning down to the realm of the dead below.
It has devoured the earth and its harvest
    and set ablaze the foundations of the mountains.

**23**
I will heap disasters upon them;
    I will exhaust my arrows against them.
**24**
They will be wasted with hunger,
    consumed by fever and bitter pestilence;
I will send against them the fangs of wild beasts,
    with the venom of vipers that crawl in the dust.

**25**
Outside, the sword will bereave;
    inside, there will be terror —
destroying young men and young women alike,
    nursing infants and gray-haired elders.

**26**
I said I would scatter them far and wide,
    I would wipe out all memory of them —

**27**
but I dreaded the taunt of the enemy,
    lest their foes misread it.
Lest they say, 'Our hand has triumphed;
    the LORD has not done all this.'"

**28**
For they are a nation lacking wisdom;
    there is no understanding in them.
**29**
If only they were wise, they would grasp this;
    they would understand what their end will be!

**30**
How can one man chase a thousand,
    or two put ten thousand to flight —
unless their Rock had sold them,
    unless the LORD had handed them over?

**31**
For their rock is not like our Rock,
    as even our enemies themselves admit.

**32**
Their vine comes from the vine of Sodom,
    from the vineyards of Gomorrah.
Their grapes are filled with poison;
    their clusters are bitter.

**33**
Their wine is the venom of serpents,
    the deadly poison of cobras.

**34**
"Is not this stored up with me,
    sealed away in my vaults?

**35**
Vengeance is mine — I will repay.
    In due time their foot will slip,
for the day of their ruin is near,
    their fate comes swiftly upon them."

**36**
For the LORD will vindicate his people
    and have compassion on his servants,
when he sees that their strength is gone
    and no one remains — slave or free.

**37**
He will say: "Where are their gods,
    the rock in whom they took refuge?

**38**
Who ate the fat of their sacrifices
    and drank the wine of their drink offerings?
Let them rise up and help you!
    Let them be your shelter!

**39**
See now: I am he — I alone!
    There is no god besides me.
I put to death and I bring to life;
    I have wounded, and I will heal —
and no one can deliver from my hand.

**40**
I lift my hand to the heavens
    and declare: As surely as I live forever —

**41**
when I sharpen my flashing sword
    and my hand grasps it in judgment,
I will take vengeance on my adversaries
    and repay those who hate me.

**42**
I will make my arrows drunk with blood —
    while my sword devours flesh:
the blood of the slain and the captives,
    from the long-haired heads of the enemy."

**43**
Rejoice, O nations, with his people!
    For he will avenge the blood of his servants;
he will take vengeance on his enemies
    and make atonement for his land and his people.

---

**44** Moses came and recited all the words of this song in the hearing of the people — he and Joshua son of Nun. **45** When Moses finished reciting all these words to all Israel, **46** he said to them: "Take to heart all the words I have solemnly declared to you this day, so that you may command your children to obey carefully every word of this instruction. **47** They are not idle words for you — they are your life! By them you will live long in the land you are crossing the Jordan to possess."

**48** On that same day the LORD said to Moses, **49** "Go up into the Abarim Mountains to Mount Nebo in Moab, across from Jericho, and view the land of Canaan I am giving the Israelites as their own possession. **50** You will die on the mountain you have ascended and be gathered to your people, just as your brother Aaron died on Mount Hor and was gathered to his people. **51** This is because both of you broke faith with me in the presence of the Israelites at the waters of Meribah Kadesh in the Desert of Zin, and you did not uphold my holiness among them. **52** Therefore you will see the land from a distance, but you will not enter the land I am giving to the people of Israel."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Overall form — Song as Witness:** Ha'azinu is one of the oldest poems in the Hebrew Bible. PNEUMA renders it with line breaks, indentation, and breath-pause structures that reflect its sung, chanted character. In the Leningrad Codex the poem appears in two columns — a visible monument to its special status. Every poetic choice here honors that intentionality.

**V. 1 — "Listen, O heavens":** Ha'azinu (Give ear, O heavens) opens with the vocative address to cosmic witnesses — heaven and earth are subpoenaed as witnesses to the covenant. PNEUMA uses "Listen" as more natural in English speech/song; "Earth, hear" keeps the parallel imperative structure.

**V. 2 — "Teaching / words / showers / rain":** The four-fold comparison (rain, dew, showers, heavy rain) describes Moses' instruction as life-giving water falling on parched ground. The dynamic rendering stretches the imagery naturally across two parallel couplets.

**V. 4 — "The Rock" (ha-Tsur):** One of the great divine epithets of the poem. PNEUMA renders ha-Tsur as "The Rock!" with an exclamation — it is a cry of faith, not just description. The word echoes through the poem as a refrain (vv. 13, 15, 18, 30, 31), providing structural unity.

**V. 6 — "Father, your Creator":** The Hebrew has av (father) and qanekha (one who acquired/created you). PNEUMA uses "Father, your Creator" to show both the relational and the cosmic dimensions — God is not merely maker but parent.

**V. 8 — "Sons of God" (bney elohim):** PNEUMA follows the Septuagint and the Dead Sea Scrolls reading here against the MT "sons of Israel." The LXX (angelon theou / huion theou) and 4QDeut both attest "sons of God" — the reading that makes better contextual sense: God apportions nations among divine beings, keeping Israel as his direct inheritance (v. 9). The MT "sons of Israel" appears to be a later theological softening.

**V. 10 — "apple of his eye" (ishon eyno):** The literal Hebrew is "the pupil of his eye" (ishon = little man, i.e., the tiny reflection seen in the pupil). "Apple of his eye" is the established English idiom for this precise concept — PNEUMA keeps it as perfect dynamic equivalence.

**V. 11 — Eagle imagery:** The eagle rousing its nest (ya'ir qino) is a stunning image of tender hovering mixed with wild power. PNEUMA renders it with active participles that breathe life into the metaphor.

**V. 15 — "Jeshurun" (Yeshurun):** The affectionate name for Israel (from yashar, "upright") is preserved — stripping it to "Israel" would lose the biting irony: the "upright one" grew fat and kicked. PNEUMA keeps the name and lets the irony land.

**V. 17 — "Demons" (shedim):** The word shedim (Akkadian: shedu, spirits/demons) is not "nothing" — they are real powers, just not the true God. PNEUMA uses "demons" for full force, not the softer "false gods."

**V. 21 — "No people" / "foolish nation":** Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:19 to describe the Gentile mission. PNEUMA renders lo-am (not-a-people) and goy naval (foolish nation) with the provocative edge intact. This is divine irony: Israel provoked God with non-gods; God will provoke Israel with non-people.

**V. 35 — "Vengeance is mine":** li naqam (vengeance belongs to me) — also quoted by Paul (Romans 12:19) and the author of Hebrews (10:30). PNEUMA renders with the terse English "Vengeance is mine — I will repay" for maximum impact and recognizability.

**V. 39 — "I am he — I alone":** ani ani hu (I, I am he) with the emphatic repetition preserved. This is one of the great monotheism declarations in the Hebrew Bible, parallel to Isaiah 43-44. PNEUMA renders the isolation and exclusivity: "I alone!"

**V. 43 — LXX addition:** The MT has a shorter version of v. 43; the LXX (Vaticanus) and Qumran texts have an expanded version including "Rejoice, O nations, with his people." PNEUMA follows the fuller text, as it is the version Paul quotes in Romans 15:10 and likely represents the older form.

**Vv. 44-52 — Prose frame:** The song ends and narrative resumes. Moses is given his final commission — to see but not enter the land. PNEUMA handles this shift from poetry to prose cleanly, letting the reader feel the transition from song to solemn instruction.

— PNEUMA
